metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `zizmor` is scoped when run under `make lint` so it does not flag `gerrit-verify.yaml`'s known out-of-scope unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6` (e.g., document a `.zizmor.yml` ignore entry for that specific file/action, or invoke zizmor with `--path .github/workflows/release.yml` in the Makefile lint target, or expand scope to fix gerrit-verify.yaml as part of this story).
findings.finding: Adding zizmor to `make lint` (global, runs on all workflows) while declaring `gerrit-verify.yaml`'s unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6` out of scope creates a mechanism deadlock: zizmor will flag the out-of-scope unpinned action in gerrit-verify.yaml, causing `make lint` to fail, which will break the Gerrit Verified gate itself. The plan says zizmor enforces 'across `release.yml`' in some places but specifies no scoping configuration (e.g., a `.zizmor.yml` with per-file ignores, or a `--path` flag limiting zizmor to `release.yml` only) that would prevent it from flagging gerrit-verify.yaml. Without this, the plan's own Gerrit gate becomes unpassable on the day this story lands — before the gerrit-verify.yaml fix lands.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev] — add zizmor and actionlint to make lint'; Why section: 'Fixing gerrit-verify.yaml's own unpinned setup-python is out of scope'; gerrit-verify.yaml:75
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) add a `.zizmor.yml` config with a `ignore` rule for `gerrit-verify.yaml`'s `setup-python` usage and document it in the Scope section, OR (b) expand the story scope to fix `gerrit-verify.yaml:75` at the same time (a one-line SHA-pin). Option (b) is simpler and eliminates the technical debt; option (a) allows the stated scope boundary to hold but requires a documented rationale comment in `.zizmor.yml`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan exactly how `zizmor` invocation under `make lint` is scoped to `release.yml` only (e.g., via a `.zizmor.toml` config file, a `--only .github/workflows/release.yml` flag, or by fixing all the unpinned non-release-workflow actions as part of this story's scope); the current plan creates a contradiction between 'add zizmor to make lint' and 'fixing gerrit-verify.yaml is out of scope'.
findings.finding: The scope-exclusion 'Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned' is false as a codebase premise: `gerrit-verify.yaml:75` (in the `require-ticket` job) has an unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6`, and multiple other workflows (`test.yml`, `_optionality.yml`, `external-integration.yml`, `mirror-guard.yml`, `eval.yml`, `reconcile-bridge.yml`, `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml`) all use unpinned `actions/checkout@v7` and `actions/setup-python@v6`. The plan then adds `zizmor` to `make lint` with no specified scope restriction. Running `zizmor` over the entire `.github/workflows/` tree (the default) under `make lint` → Gerrit Verified gate would generate findings for all those workflows, breaking the gate. The plan says it touches 'only `release.yml`' but provides no `zizmor` config, `--only` flag, or `.zizmor.toml` scoping that confines zizmor to `release.yml` specifically.
findings.location: Why section ('gerrit-verify.yaml:75 still has an unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6`'); Scope section ('Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned'); AC3 ('`zizmor` + `actionlint` run under `make lint`')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Add a `.zizmor.toml` configuration file that limits `zizmor` to only `.github/workflows/release.yml` (or explicitly enumerate all workflows to include), so that unpinned actions in out-of-scope workflows (e.g., `gerrit-verify.yaml:75`, `test.yml`, `_optionality.yml`) do not cause Gerrit gate failures.' OR expand scope to pin all workflows.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `zizmor` in `make lint` will be scoped so it only enforces SHA-pins on `release.yml` (e.g. via a `zizmor --config .zizmor.toml` that ignores non-release workflows, or by passing `release.yml` as an explicit argument), OR expand the plan's scope to also SHA-pin all other `.github/workflows/*.yml` files in the same story.
findings.finding: `zizmor` added to `make lint` would scan ALL `.github/workflows/*.yml` files, not just `release.yml`, but 21+ other workflow files contain unpinned `actions/checkout@v7` and `actions/setup-python@v6` references (e.g. `test.yml:36`, `test.yml:37`, `gerrit-verify.yaml:75`, `_optionality.yml:55`, `mirror-guard.yml:36`, `external-integration.yml:35`, `eval.yml:45`, `codeql.yml:40`, etc.). The plan says fixing non-release workflows is 'out of scope' but does not specify that `zizmor` in `make lint` will be restricted to `release.yml` only. This means `make lint` (which is called by the Gerrit Verified gate via `gerrit-verify.yaml`) would immediately fail on every change submitted after this story lands, blocking the entire team's workflow until every other workflow is also pinned.
findings.location: Scope section / `Makefile` + `pyproject.toml [dev]` — add `zizmor` and `actionlint` to `make lint`; Out of scope clause: 'pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml` (explicit path argument) to the Makefile `lint` target instead of `zizmor .github/workflows/` or a bare `zizmor`, so only `release.yml` is checked. Document this deliberate scoping decision in the Scope section with a note that other workflows will be pinned in a follow-on story.

findings.finding: The plan's scope-exclusion claim 'Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned' is factually false for the broader workflow corpus: `test.yml`, `mirror-guard.yml`, `external-integration.yml`, `_optionality.yml`, `reconcile-bridge.yml`, `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml`, `verify-identity.yaml`, `eval.yml`, `eval-plan-review-container.yml`, `terraform-drift.yml` all use mutable `actions/checkout@v7` and/or `actions/setup-python@v6` tags. If `zizmor` is added to `make lint` and scans all `.github/workflows/*.yml` files (its natural default scope), it will fail on these unpinned references and break the Gerrit Verified gate for every change — contradicting the plan's claim that only `release.yml` needs fixing.
findings.location: Out of scope section; Scope section (Makefile + shared lint config bullet); Why section ('the generic action-security checks are not yet enforced on release.yml')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, specify the exact zizmor invocation (e.g. `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml`) so it audits only the targeted file, consistent with the stated out-of-scope boundary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact zizmor invocation in the Makefile addition (e.g., `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml` or a `zizmor.toml` config that restricts the scan to release.yml), so the out-of-scope gerrit-verify.yaml unpinned action does not cause make lint to fail.
findings.finding: zizmor is added to `make lint` (which runs under the Gerrit Verified gate on ALL workflows), but gerrit-verify.yaml:75 has an unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6` that the plan explicitly declares out of scope to fix. If zizmor scans all workflow files in `.github/workflows/`, it will flag this unpinned reference and break `make lint` — blocking every Gerrit Verified vote. The AC language ('zizmor reports no findings on `release.yml`') implies zizmor is scoped to a single file, but neither the plan body nor any Scope item specifies a `--config`, path argument, or `zizmor release.yml` invocation that would limit the scan to release.yml only.
findings.location: Scope section ('Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev] — add zizmor and actionlint to make lint'), AC3 ('zizmor reports no findings on release.yml'), gerrit-verify.yaml:75
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope/Makefile bullet, write: `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml` (scoped to the single file this story owns) rather than `zizmor` with no argument. Document that gerrit-verify.yaml's unpinned action is tracked separately and zizmor is intentionally scoped to release.yml for this story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the zizmor invocation scope contradiction: explicitly state in the Scope and AC whether `make lint` invokes `zizmor` on all workflows or only `release.yml`, and if only `release.yml`, add a note that gerrit-verify.yaml will be addressed in a follow-on story to avoid confusion about the gate's coverage.
findings.finding: The plan adds `zizmor` to `make lint` (which runs under the Gerrit Verified gate on all workflows) but simultaneously declares that fixing `gerrit-verify.yaml`'s unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6` (:75) is **out of scope**. By default `zizmor` scans all workflow files in `.github/workflows/`, so if invoked without a `--file release.yml` scope restriction, it will report findings on `gerrit-verify.yaml` and fail `make lint`, breaking the Gerrit gate for every subsequent change. The AC says only '`zizmor` reports no findings on `release.yml`' (implying file-scoped), but the Scope and What sections never specify that zizmor is restricted to `release.yml` alone. An implementer reading Scope would add zizmor to make lint globally; an implementer reading the AC would scope it to release.yml — two incompatible implementations.
findings.location: Scope (make lint paragraph) vs. AC bullet 3 vs. Why section ('Fixing gerrit-verify.yaml's own unpinned setup-python is out of scope')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope make-lint paragraph: 'Invoke `zizmor` with `--file .github/workflows/release.yml` in `make lint` for this story; a follow-on story will extend coverage to `gerrit-verify.yaml` once its unpinned action is fixed.' Update AC bullet 3 to say 'zizmor is invoked with `--file .github/workflows/release.yml`' to remove the ambiguity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `pip-tools` (with a pinned exact version) to `pyproject.toml [dev]` or provide a pinned Makefile target that installs it (analogous to the `actionlint` download target), so `pip-compile --generate-hashes` is available at a deterministic version for lock-file regeneration.
findings.finding: `pip-compile` (pip-tools) is named as the canonical lock-file regeneration tool throughout the plan's Scope, ACs, and docs grep-check, but pip-tools has no installation path specified for the developer workflow. The plan adds `zizmor` to `pyproject.toml [dev]` and `actionlint` via a Makefile download target, but `pip-compile` is neither added to `pyproject.toml [dev]` nor given a pinned Makefile install target. A developer following the docs to regenerate `.github/release-requirements.txt` would need to obtain `pip-compile` independently, with no pinned version — undermining the story's own 'update pins through reviewed changes, not implicit resolution' principle.
findings.location: Scope section / `Makefile` + `pyproject.toml [dev]`; AC #3: 'generated by `pip-compile --generate-hashes`'; docs AC: 'the command to regenerate the lock file (`pip-compile --generate-hashes …`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit install path for pip-tools in the plan (e.g., add `pip-tools` to `pyproject.toml [dev]` alongside zizmor, or specify a `pipx install pip-tools==<version>` step in the Makefile), and either commit to one canonical tool (`pip-compile` or `uv pip compile`) or make both the docs grep-check substring and the AC command tool-agnostic.
findings.finding: pip-tools (`pip-compile`) is the plan's canonical lock-file generation command (hardcoded in Scope, AC3, and the docs grep-check literal `pip-compile --generate-hashes`), but no install path for pip-tools is specified anywhere in the plan — unlike zizmor (pinned in pyproject.toml [dev]) and actionlint (Makefile prerequisite + SHA-256 download). pip-tools is entirely absent from pyproject.toml [dev], the Makefile, and every workflow. A developer following the plan to regenerate the lock file has no stated way to obtain pip-compile.
findings.location: Scope section ('pip-compile --generate-hashes --output-file=...'), AC3 ('generated by pip-compile --generate-hashes'), AC7 ('docs AC grep check: pip-compile --generate-hashes')
findings.suggested_fix: Add `pip-tools>=7` (or a pinned version) to `pyproject.toml [dev]`, so `pip install -e '.[dev]'` makes `pip-compile` available — mirroring how zizmor is handled. Alternatively, commit to `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` (uv is already the plan's other named alternative and requires no separate install if uv is already present in CI) and update the docs grep-check substring accordingly.

findings.finding: The 'What' section and AC3 are inconsistent on whether `--no-deps` is part of the hash-locked install command. The What section says `pip install --require-hashes -r .github/release-requirements.txt` (no `--no-deps`), while AC3 specifies `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt`. Omitting `--no-deps` means pip may resolve and install transitive dependencies not listed in the lock file without hash-checking them, silently bypassing the supply-chain guarantee the plan is building.
findings.location: What section — 'Hatchling enforcement — decision' paragraph; AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Update the What section to read: `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt` and add a parenthetical: '(--no-deps is required so pip does not resolve and install unlocked transitive dependencies that bypass the hash check).'

findings.finding: The plan names `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (pip-tools) as the canonical lock-file generation command throughout Scope, AC3, and the docs grep check, but `pip-tools` is entirely absent from `pyproject.toml [dev]` and from every workflow. No installation path for `pip-compile` is specified, leaving the lock-file generation step without an executable tool.
findings.location: Scope section ('.github/release-requirements.txt (new)'); AC3; docs AC (grep for 'pip-compile --generate-hashes')
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"pip-tools>=7"` to `pyproject.toml [dev]` dependencies, or document `pipx install pip-tools` as a one-time developer prerequisite in the new 'Pinning' section of `docs/releasing.md`.

findings.finding: `pip-compile` (pip-tools) is named as the canonical lock-file generation command throughout the Scope, ACs, and the docs grep check (`grep -q 'pip-compile --generate-hashes'`), but pip-tools is entirely absent from `pyproject.toml [dev]`, any workflow, and the Makefile. No installation path or version pin for `pip-compile` itself is named in the plan.
findings.location: Scope: `.github/release-requirements.txt (new … compiled … via pip-compile --generate-hashes …)`; AC3: `pip-compile --generate-hashes --output-file=…`; AC7 docs grep check.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in the plan that `mcp_verify` must declare `needs: publish` (or equivalent) so the ordering guarantee from the current `mcp_registry: needs: publish` is preserved in the split-job design, and specify which job hosts the 'Wait for PyPI' polling step.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the new `mcp_verify` + `mcp_registry` job split replaces the existing `mcp_registry` job (which declares `needs: publish` at `release.yml:111`), but never specifies that `mcp_verify` must inherit that `needs: publish` dependency. Without it, `mcp_verify` could run concurrently with or before `publish`, downloading a publisher binary and uploading it as an artifact before the PyPI publish job has succeeded — allowing the OIDC-privileged `mcp_registry` to run even on a failed PyPI publish.
findings.location: What section ('split the job in two: `mcp_verify` … and `mcp_registry` (`needs: mcp_verify`)'); Scope section ('split the MCP publisher into an unprivileged `mcp_verify` job + an OIDC-privileged `mcp_registry` job')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope/What section: '`mcp_verify` declares `needs: publish` to preserve the existing ordering guarantee (MCP publish only runs after a successful PyPI publish); the PyPI-wait polling step moves into `mcp_verify`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the new `mcp_verify` job must declare `needs: publish` (or equivalent) so the MCP pipeline remains ordered after PyPI publication, and explicitly assign the 'Wait for PyPI to serve this version' polling step to one of the two new jobs.
findings.finding: The plan creates a new `mcp_verify` job and states `mcp_registry needs: mcp_verify`, but never specifies what `needs:` `mcp_verify` itself should declare. The existing `mcp_registry` job has `needs: publish` (release.yml line 111), meaning it waits for PyPI publication before running. If the new `mcp_verify` job doesn't also declare `needs: publish`, it could start concurrently with (or before) the `publish` job, violating the ordering guarantee that PyPI has the artifact before the MCP registry check runs.
findings.location: Scope section / `.github/workflows/release.yml`; What section — 'splitting the job in two: mcp_verify … and mcp_registry (needs: mcp_verify)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `needs: publish` to the new `mcp_verify` job declaration in the plan, mirroring the existing `mcp_registry` dependency. Move the 'Wait for PyPI' step to `mcp_verify` (before the sha256sum check) since `mcp_verify` now runs first.

findings.finding: The plan states the split `mcp_registry needs: mcp_verify`, but never specifies what `mcp_verify` itself `needs:`. The current `mcp_registry` job has `needs: publish` (release.yml:111), meaning it runs after PyPI publish. If `mcp_verify` does not inherit `needs: publish` (or an equivalent sequencing dependency), it will run before PyPI publish completes — making the 'Wait for PyPI' polling loop in the job meaningless and potentially racing with the publish job.
findings.location: What section ('Security contract — structural job split'); Scope section (release.yml bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'mcp_verify: needs: publish' to the job-split specification in the plan's What section and Scope section.

findings.finding: The plan splits the existing `mcp_registry` job (which currently has `needs: publish` at `release.yml` line 111) into `mcp_verify` + `mcp_registry`, but never specifies that `mcp_verify` must inherit the `needs: publish` dependency. Without this, `mcp_verify` would start in parallel with the `publish` job, potentially downloading and verifying the MCP publisher binary before the PyPI publish gate that the MCP registry also depends on.
findings.location: Scope: `.github/workflows/release.yml — … split the MCP publisher into an unprivileged mcp_verify job + an OIDC-privileged mcp_registry job`; What section: 'mcp_registry (`needs: mcp_verify`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Set `mcp_verify: needs: publish` (or `needs: [publish]`) in the new job definition, keeping the existing sequencing invariant. The 'Wait for PyPI' step should move to `mcp_verify` since it gates the point where the registry can be published, not where the binary is executed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify before implementation whether `modelcontextprotocol/registry` publishes a checksums.txt (or equivalent) alongside its release tarballs, and resolve the plan to a single deterministic implementation path (hard-coded SHA-256 if no upstream checksums file exists, with a comment; or specify that the checksums.txt is fetched and verified against a pinned signing key if one exists).
findings.finding: The plan states 'Prefer an upstream checksums.txt if modelcontextprotocol/registry ships one; otherwise hard-code the SHA-256' but does not verify at plan time whether `modelcontextprotocol/registry` actually ships a checksums file alongside its release archives. This unresolved conditional drives a materially different implementation: fetching a checksums.txt at workflow runtime introduces a new mutable network call inside `mcp_verify` (potentially downloadable from a compromised CDN), while hard-coding the SHA-256 has no such exposure. The choice should be resolved in the plan, not left to the implementer.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP publisher — download → verify → execute' bullet, arch note
findings.suggested_fix: Check the https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry/releases page; if no checksums.txt is published, remove the conditional and commit to hard-coded SHA-256 with a rationale comment — this is the simpler, more auditable path regardless.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` scope entry (and optionally in `docs/releasing.md`) where the base allowlist is declared and how to extend it — e.g. a top-of-file constant `BASELINE_PACKAGES = frozenset({...})` with a comment pointing to the runner image release notes, so the update path is discoverable.
findings.finding: The base allowlist (`pip`, `setuptools`, `wheel`) inside `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` is hardcoded with no plan-specified location or procedure for updating it. If the `ubuntu-latest` runner adds another pre-installed package (e.g. `packaging`, which pip already depends on), or if the project's own venv setup changes, a developer must find and edit the hardcoded list inside the script — but the plan provides no doc on where the list lives or how to extend it.
findings.location: Scope — `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py`; Acceptance Criteria — 'modulo the documented `pip`/`setuptools`/`wheel` base allowlist'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope entry: '`BASELINE_PACKAGES` is a top-of-file `frozenset` constant in `check_build_env_locked.py`; extend it by adding the package name and updating the comment with the runner image version that introduced it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the pip-tools choice over `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` (the plan's own named alternative), or update the docs AC grep target and Scope to accept either tool's output format — and confirm pip-tools is the right single-call-site dependency to introduce.
findings.finding: pip-tools (`pip-compile`) is introduced as the canonical lock-file generation command with zero existing call-sites in the codebase (absent from pyproject.toml [dev], Makefile, and every workflow), yet the plan commits to it in the Scope section, AC3, and hard-codes `pip-compile --generate-hashes` as a mandatory grep substring in the docs AC. The plan's own 'What' section lists `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` as a co-equal alternative, but ACs and the docs grep target lock in pip-tools vocabulary, making the choice irreversible at the AC level. This is a single-call-site tool dependency introduced without justification for choosing pip-tools over the already-mentioned uv alternative.
findings.location: Scope section (.github/release-requirements.txt bullet); AC3; docs AC (grep substring `pip-compile --generate-hashes`)
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) add a rationale sentence to the Scope explaining why pip-tools is preferred over uv (e.g., 'uv is not in the dev toolchain; pip-tools is pip-installable without a Rust binary'); or (b) update the docs AC grep target to a tool-agnostic substring like `--generate-hashes` (present in both pip-compile and uv pip compile output) so the lock file command can be regenerated with either tool without breaking the docs test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out procedure for the `--no-isolation` build approach: either in `docs/releasing.md` or the Scope, document what a releaser should do if the hash-locked env fails to build (e.g., regenerate the lock with `pip-compile` for the runner platform, or the conditions under which reverting to isolated builds is permissible and how to re-secure it).
findings.finding: The plan changes the release workflow's build invocation from the existing `python -m build` (isolated env) to `python -m build --no-isolation` against a hash-locked pre-installed venv. This is an explicit justified choice, but the plan provides **no rollback or back-out path** if the `--no-isolation` approach causes a build failure in production (e.g., a transitive dep missing from the lock, a platform-specific wheel not in the lock). The plan mitigates forward (with `check_build_env_locked.py` and `--require-hashes`), but if a release is blocked by this mechanism, the documented recovery path is absent — the releaser has no documented procedure to temporarily revert to isolated builds or to diagnose the failure.
findings.location: What section ('Hatchling enforcement — decision') and Scope ('.github/release-requirements.txt (new)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the `docs/releasing.md` Pinning section AC to require at minimum one sentence (or a subsection) documenting the OIDC job-split rationale — specifically that `id-token: write` is job-scoped (reachable from step 1), so step ordering alone cannot isolate it, and the `mcp_verify`/`mcp_registry` boundary is the structural enforcement — and add a corresponding AC substring assertion in `test_release_workflow_pins.py`.
findings.finding: The `docs/releasing.md` Pinning section AC specifies only three literal substrings (`pip-compile --generate-hashes`, `MCP publisher`, `full-SHA`), none of which require documenting the OIDC job-split architectural rationale. The structural decision that `id-token: write` is a job-level permission reachable from step 1 — and therefore requires a job boundary, not step ordering — is a novel, non-obvious architectural decision that is explained in the plan body but is not required to appear in any durable artifact (doc or ADR).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `docs/releasing.md` bullet; Scope — `docs/releasing.md` entry
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fourth required substring to the docs AC, e.g. `'id-token: write is a job-level permission'` or `'mcp_verify job holds no id-token'`, and add `assert 'id-token: write is a job-level permission' in pinning_section` to `test_release_workflow_pins.py` check (d).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan-specified procedure (or a CI assertion) to keep the `zizmor` version in `pyproject.toml [dev]` and in `.github/release-requirements.txt` in sync — e.g. a `make lint` check that compares `pip show zizmor` against the lock's pinned version, or a note in `docs/releasing.md` Pinning section that both pins must be updated together when upgrading `zizmor`.
findings.finding: `zizmor` is pinned in two separate places — `pyproject.toml [dev]` (for `make lint` in the Gerrit gate) and `.github/release-requirements.txt` (hash-pinned for release-lint usage) — but the plan specifies no procedure or tooling to keep these two pins in sync. A routine `pip-compile` update of the release lock could advance one pin while leaving the other stale, causing the Gerrit gate and the release workflow to run different `zizmor` versions silently.
findings.location: Scope — 'Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev]' entry; Acceptance Criteria — `zizmor` + `actionlint` AC bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the docs/releasing.md Pinning section AC: 'documents that the zizmor version in pyproject.toml [dev] and .github/release-requirements.txt must match, and the command to update both atomically.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--connect-timeout 30 --max-time 120` (or equivalent) to every `curl` invocation in the plan (MCP publisher download and actionlint Makefile prerequisite) so a hung upstream fails the step within a bounded window with a clear non-zero exit.
findings.finding: The curl downloads for the MCP publisher (`mcp_verify` job) and the `actionlint` binary (Makefile prerequisite) specify no `--connect-timeout` or `--max-time` flag. If the upstream host is slow or silently hangs, the CI job will block until GitHub Actions' default job timeout (6 hours), stalling the entire release pipeline or Gerrit Verified gate with no diagnostic signal.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher download steps 1-3; Scope — Makefile prerequisite for actionlint
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `curl -fsSL "<url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz` with `curl -fsSL --connect-timeout 30 --max-time 120 "<url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz` in both the workflow and the Makefile prerequisite. Document the chosen timeouts in the rationale comment alongside the SHA-256.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the `check_build_env_locked.py` unit test to cover: (a) a fixture where an installed package version differs from the locked version (assert non-zero exit), (b) a fixture where an allowlisted package (`pip`/`setuptools`/`wheel`) is present but absent from the lock (assert zero exit), and (c) a fixture where a non-allowlisted package is absent from the lock (assert non-zero exit, confirming the allowlist is name-exact).
findings.finding: The unit test for `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` covers only the 'extra package absent from lock' failure mode; the 'version-mismatch' failure path (installed version differs from locked version) and the 'allowlist pass' behavior (pip/setuptools/wheel present but correctly allowed) are not explicitly tested, leaving gaps in the script's two primary security-enforcement branches.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC for `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` / `tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py` check (h)
findings.suggested_fix: Add three fixture pairs to the unit test: (1) lock has `foo==1.0`, freeze has `foo==1.1` → expect exit 1; (2) lock has no `pip` entry, freeze has `pip==23.0` → expect exit 0 (allowlist pass); (3) lock has no `bar` entry, freeze has `bar==2.0` → expect exit 1 (non-allowlist extra). This exercises all three branches of the script's comparison logic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether actionlint should follow the existing git-cliff convention (version-pin in Makefile + `pipx install actionlint@<version>` error guidance) rather than introducing a new curl+SHA-256 download Makefile target — or explicitly document why the mcp-publisher download pattern is preferred here over pipx.
findings.finding: The plan installs `actionlint` (a Go binary) via 'a pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification in a `Makefile` prerequisite' — a pattern not present in the existing Makefile. The Makefile's established convention for standalone external binaries is a version-check + `pipx install` instruction (see `git-cliff`: `GIT_CLIFF_VERSION` constant + `@command -v git-cliff` check + error directing the user to `pipx install git-cliff==$(GIT_CLIFF_VERSION)`). Introducing a new `curl`+`sha256sum`+install Makefile download target for actionlint is a second installation pattern for the same category of tool — violating the existing convention and applying the mcp-publisher download pattern (designed for CI release workflows) to a local developer lint tool.
findings.location: Scope section (Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev] bullet); AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the git-cliff pattern: add `ACTIONLINT_VERSION := X.Y.Z` to the Makefile, check `command -v actionlint` in the lint target, and emit `error: actionlint not installed — run: go install github.com/rhysd/actionlint/cmd/actionlint@v$(ACTIONLINT_VERSION)` (or pipx if a pipx-installable wrapper exists). This keeps a single installation convention for standalone binary tools.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether updating the `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` reference in `docs/releasing.md:45` (Supply-chain attestations section) is in scope for this story or an adjacent one, and add it explicitly to the scope list to avoid post-land documentation drift.
findings.finding: The plan's scope-exclusion for `docs/releasing.md` is 'this story owns the Pinning section only', but after this plan SHA-pins `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` in `release.yml`, the existing 'Supply-chain attestations' section of `docs/releasing.md` (line 45) still quotes the mutable tag `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` verbatim. The doc will be factually wrong immediately after this plan lands, yet updating that reference is explicitly excluded from scope.
findings.location: Scope section ('Section ownership: this story owns the "Pinning" section only'); docs/releasing.md:45

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address whether the `hatchling>=1.18` floor in `pyproject.toml [dev]` should be exact-pinned to match the release lock, or document explicitly that developer builds are permitted to differ in Hatchling version from the release lock.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `hatchling` is declared as a bare unversioned string in `pyproject.toml:1-3` (verified correct: `requires = ["hatchling"]`) and that the `--no-isolation` strategy governs Hatchling's version via the pre-installed hash-locked env. However, `pyproject.toml [dev]` already declares `hatchling>=1.18` as a dev dep (verified at `pyproject.toml:152`). The plan says `pyproject.toml` has 'no change to `[build-system]` requires', but does not address whether the `hatchling>=1.18` floor in `[dev]` is also updated to an exact pin consistent with the release lock — leaving a version mismatch possible if the `[dev]` floor and the release lock pin different versions.
findings.location: Scope section ('`pyproject.toml` — no change to `[build-system]` requires'); What section ('Hatchling enforcement — decision'); pyproject.toml:152

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Close the security contract in `mcp_registry` by adding a re-digest step (`echo "<pinned-sha256>  mcp-publisher" | sha256sum -c --strict`) after `download-artifact` and before `chmod +x` / execution, or explicitly acknowledge in the plan that the re-digest is intentionally omitted and state the trust model for GitHub's internal artifact channel.
findings.finding: The plan's stated security contract — 'the OIDC-privileged job never downloads or holds an unverified binary' — is not fully closed by the mechanism as specified. `mcp_verify` verifies the SHA-256 of the *downloaded archive* (`mcp-publisher.tar.gz`), then extracts the binary and uploads the *extracted binary* as a GitHub Actions artifact. `mcp_registry` downloads that artifact and executes `./mcp-publisher login` with `id-token: write` in scope. No re-digest of the extracted binary occurs in `mcp_registry`. The plan requires `chmod +x mcp-publisher` (correctly noting that upload-artifact drops the exec bit) but not a re-verification of the binary's SHA-256 before execution. The GitHub internal artifact channel is not publicly attacker-controlled, so this is a defense-in-depth gap rather than a critical attack surface, but the stated contract ('never ... unverified binary') is not fully delivered by the specified mechanism.
findings.location: What section: 'Security contract — structural job split (not step ordering)'; AC5: 'the OIDC-privileged mcp_registry job needs: mcp_verify and consumes only the verified artifact — so the OIDC scope is never co-resident with an unverified binary'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `mcp_registry` job spec: after `actions/download-artifact`, insert `echo "<pinned-sha256>  mcp-publisher" | sha256sum -c --strict` before `chmod +x mcp-publisher`. The pinned SHA-256 here is the SHA-256 of the *extracted binary* (not the archive), computed once at pin time. Store it alongside the archive SHA-256 as a second workflow variable. This makes the security contract literally true: mcp_registry executes only a binary whose digest matches the value reviewed at pin time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why `pip-compile` (pip-tools) is chosen over `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` (or switch to uv if already in the toolchain), and add pip-tools to `pyproject.toml [dev]` or provide an explicit install path so `pip-compile` is available to developers.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (pip-tools) as the canonical lock-file generation tool — committed in the Scope, AC3, and the required docs substring — but pip-tools has zero existing footprint in this repo (absent from `pyproject.toml [dev]`, the Makefile, and every workflow), while the `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` alternative (from astral-sh/uv, the rapidly-established modern standard for this use case) is acknowledged only as a parenthetical alternative ('or `uv pip compile --generate-hashes`') with no comparative justification for why pip-tools is preferred. This is a new external tool integration chosen without documented prior-art evaluation.
findings.location: What section — 'Create a reviewed release lock/constraints file' bullet; Scope — `.github/release-requirements.txt`; AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documented trade-off comparison in the plan (or a design note in `docs/releasing.md`) explaining why `--no-isolation` + `check_build_env_locked.py` is preferred over the standard `[build-system] requires = ["hatchling==<exact>"]` pin, so the architectural choice is traceable.
findings.finding: The `--no-isolation` build strategy is a deliberate deviation from PEP 517's standard isolated build mode, chosen to allow Hatchling version enforcement via the pre-installed hash-locked env. The plan justifies this ('avoids editing pyproject.toml [build-system]') but does not compare it against the well-established alternative: pinning `hatchling==<exact-version>` directly in `[build-system] requires` in `pyproject.toml`, which is the canonical, community-standard approach for locking the build backend. The `--no-isolation` path introduces complexity (the bespoke `check_build_env_locked.py` guard to close the ambient-package hole) that the standard approach does not require.
findings.location: What section — 'Hatchling enforcement — decision' bullet; Scope — `pyproject.toml` note; AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `zizmor`'s integrity is verified on the Gerrit gate path (the primary enforcement path) — either by extending the Gerrit gate's dev-install to use a hash-locked constraints file for `zizmor` specifically, or by documenting that the Gerrit gate installs from `release-requirements.txt` (with `--require-hashes`) rather than bare `[dev]` extras — so the security enforcer is hash-pinned on every path where it runs, not only on the release path.
findings.finding: The plan mandates hash-pinning `zizmor` in the release lock (release path) but specifies only a version pin in `pyproject.toml [dev]` for the Gerrit pre-merge gate path (`pip install -e '.[dev]'`). The Gerrit gate's `pip install -e '.[dev]'` does not use `--require-hashes`, so `zizmor` — the security enforcer whose entire purpose is to reject unpinned actions — is itself installed from PyPI without hash verification on the primary enforcement path.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev]' paragraph; AC line 3 ('zizmor + actionlint run under make lint')
findings.suggested_fix: Add `zizmor` to `.github/release-requirements.txt` (already planned) and update the Gerrit gate's dev-install step in `gerrit-verify.yaml` to install `zizmor` from the hash-locked requirements file (e.g., `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt` for the linter subset) rather than from the bare `[dev]` extras. Alternatively, introduce a separate `zizmor-requirements.txt` compiled with `pip-compile --generate-hashes` containing only `zizmor` and its transitive deps, installed with `--require-hashes` before `make lint` in the Gerrit gate — and document this in the Scope section.

findings.finding: The unit test for `check_build_env_locked.py` covers the 'extra package' failure case but does not explicitly cover the 'version-mismatched package' failure case, even though the AC and guard-script spec call out both as required failure modes for this security-critical script.
findings.location: Scope: `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py`; AC: 'fails if any installed package is absent from the lock or version-mismatched'; Scope: 'a unit test over a fixture lock+freeze pair to confirm it passes on a clean env and fails on an extra package'
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_check_build_env_locked.py`, add a parametrized case: lock says `hatchling==1.20.0`, freeze says `hatchling==1.21.0`; assert script returns non-zero. Pair with the existing 'extra package' case and a 'clean env' pass case.

findings.finding: The plan adds zizmor + actionlint to `make lint` (Makefile) but specifies no installation path for these tools. `zizmor` is a Rust binary and `actionlint` is a Go binary — neither is a pip package and neither appears in `pyproject.toml [dev]`. The Gerrit Verified gate installs deps via `pip install -e '.[dev]'` (gerrit-verify.yaml:150), so these tools would be absent when `make lint` runs in CI, silently skipping the enforcement they are meant to provide.
findings.location: Scope section / Makefile bullet; AC3 ('zizmor + actionlint run under make lint')

findings.finding: `zizmor` and `actionlint` are added to `make lint` (a Makefile edit), but neither tool has an installation path: they are absent from `pyproject.toml [dev]`, absent from every workflow's install step, and absent from the `Makefile` itself. `gerrit-verify.yaml` installs the dev environment via `pip install -e '.[dev]'` before running `make lint` — so the expanded `make lint` will fail with 'command not found' unless an install path is specified.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Makefile + shared lint config — add `zizmor` (version+hash-pinned in the release lock) and `actionlint` to `make lint`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `zizmor` and `actionlint` to the `[dev]` extra in `pyproject.toml` (or install via the release lock if they should not be in the project dev deps), and document any version pins. Alternatively, invoke them as pre-commit hooks in `.pre-commit-config.yaml` rather than directly in `make lint`.

findings.finding: The plan adds `zizmor` and `actionlint` to `make lint` (Makefile + AC3) but provides no installation path for these tools in the `make lint` execution environment. The Makefile's `lint` target currently runs only `ruff`, which is a `pyproject.toml [dev]` dependency installed via `pip install -e '.[dev]'`. `gerrit-verify.yaml` (the Gerrit Verified gate) installs with `pip install -e '.[dev]'` then runs `make lint`. The plan states 'zizmor is itself version+hash-pinned in the release lock' — but `.github/release-requirements.txt` is the *release* lock, not the dev/CI environment. Neither `zizmor` nor `actionlint` are added to `pyproject.toml [dev]` or any other install path that would make them available when `make lint` runs in `gerrit-verify.yaml` or local dev. As written, `make lint` would fail with 'command not found: zizmor'.
findings.location: Scope section ('Makefile + shared lint config — add zizmor … and actionlint to make lint'); AC3 ('zizmor + actionlint run under make lint'); What section ('zizmor is itself version+hash-pinned in the release lock')
findings.suggested_fix: Add `zizmor` and `actionlint` to `pyproject.toml`'s `[dev]` optional-dependencies list (pinned to exact versions), so `pip install -e '.[dev]'` installs them and `make lint` can invoke them. Update the Makefile `lint` target to call `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml` and `actionlint .github/workflows/release.yml` after the ruff checks. Separately, the release lock can still pin zizmor for use in the release workflow if desired, but the dev/CI install path must be independent.

findings.finding: AC check (h) in test_release_workflow_pins.py uses the phrase 'pip freeze diffed against the lock', directly contradicting the plan's emphatic design decision that the check is a 'deterministic parsed check, not a text diff of the two file formats' and that 'a raw text diff of the two formats would be meaningless'. An implementer reading only the AC could implement a raw text diff, which the plan explicitly rejects.
findings.location: AC — test_release_workflow_pins.py check (h); What section — 'Close the ambient-package hole with a parsed comparison'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'pip freeze diffed against the lock' in check (h) with 'pip freeze parsed (lowercased name→version) compared against the lock's name==version entries (skipping --hash/comment lines) — not a raw text diff'.

findings.finding: AC check (h) in `test_release_workflow_pins.py` is described as 'an ambient-package guard step (pip freeze diffed against the lock) is present' — the phrase 'pip freeze diffed against the lock' directly contradicts the plan's emphatic design decision in the What section and AC5 that the check is a 'deterministic parsed check, not a text diff of the two file formats'. An implementer reading check (h) in the Scope section will implement a raw diff (which the plan explicitly says is broken/meaningless), while an implementer reading AC5 will implement the parsed comparison. This instruction-locality antipattern means the test assertion and the guard script can diverge silently.
findings.location: Scope section: `tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py` check (h) description; also AC5 ('a raw text diff of the two formats would be meaningless')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section's check (h) description, change 'an ambient-package guard step (pip freeze diffed against the lock) is present' to 'an ambient-package guard step invoking scripts/check_build_env_locked.py (parsed name→version comparison, not a raw diff) is present in the build job'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the evidence-consolidation job explicitly in the plan (e.g., `evidence_consolidate`) so that `test_release_workflow_pins.py` check (f) can assert its presence by name in `release.yml` without executor invention.
findings.finding: The consolidation job that concatenates evidence fragments into release-evidence.txt is described but never named, leaving test_release_workflow_pins.py check (f)'s assertion about 'a consolidation job downloads + concatenates them' under-specified: an executor writing the test cannot target a specific job name without inventing one, and a reviewer cannot verify the test targets the right job.
findings.location: What section ('a final aggregation step downloads all fragments'); Scope / test_release_workflow_pins.py check (f)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope bullet for test_release_workflow_pins.py: 'check (f) additionally asserts that a job named `evidence_consolidate` (or the chosen canonical name) appears in release.yml and contains a step that downloads all three fragment artifacts and writes release-evidence.txt.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC that exercises the modified release.yml end-to-end in GitHub Actions — e.g., a `workflow_dispatch`-triggered dry-run job on a `test/pin-*` branch that runs through the full job graph (using `--dry-run` for twine/mcp-publisher publish steps and TestPyPI for the build job) and asserts the workflow completes with exit 0, or explicitly defer this to a named follow-on story with a rationale comment in the AC list.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion requires a trial execution of the modified release.yml in GitHub Actions (e.g., a workflow_dispatch dry-run, a TestPyPI publish, or a scheduled test release). Every proving command is either a static lint (zizmor / actionlint / make lint), a YAML-parse unit test (test_release_workflow_pins.py), or a local fixture test (test_mcp_publisher_pin.py). The actual cutover path — pinned actions resolving and executing on GitHub's runners, hash-locked pip install succeeding in the Actions env, mcp_verify uploading an artifact that mcp_registry can download and chmod+x — is never exercised end-to-end by any named AC.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all ACs); plan's E6(f) obligation
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: '[ ] A `workflow_dispatch` trigger on a `test/pin-*` branch executes `release.yml` end-to-end (build jobs use `--dry-run` / TestPyPI; mcp_registry uses a `--dry-run` flag or a test registry endpoint); the run completes without error, confirming pinned action SHAs resolve, `--require-hashes` install succeeds in the ubuntu-latest environment, and the mcp_verify→mcp_registry artifact handoff works. If a live dry-run is impractical, document the rationale and name the follow-on story that will perform it.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
